title: Namiūṇa Bhayahara Stotra (Fifth Smaraṇa)
summary: This is a 25-verse hymn of praise to Pārśvanātha, the 23rd Tīrthaṅkara, recited to remove fear. It works through a vivid list of life-threatening dangers — disease, drowning, fire, snakebite, robbers, wild animals, and battle — and promises that those who remember Pārśva's feet or his name come through each one unharmed. Hidden inside it is a secret eighteen-syllable mantra, and it closes by recalling how Pārśva stayed serene in meditation even while the demon Kamaṭha attacked him. It is called 'Bhayahara' (fear-remover) because its whole purpose is to dissolve dread and grant protection.
ritualPlacement: It is the fifth of the Nine Smaraṇas (Nava Smaraṇa), the fixed set of nine protective stotras recited within the Pākṣika (Pakkhī) pratikramaṇa in this Anchalgachchha (Vidhipaksha) tradition. Because Cāumāsī and Sāṁvatsarī carry no independent text of their own — they reproduce the Pākṣika sequence with only the word-substitution 'pakkhī → cāumāsī / saṁvatsarī' and a longer kāyotsarga — this Smaraṇa recurs unchanged in those two pratikramaṇas as well. As a Smaraṇa it belongs to the protective-recitation portion, not to the six core Āvaśyakas.
authorAndSource: Traditionally ascribed to Ācārya Mānatuṅga(sūri), whose name is woven into the poem itself: verse 21 prays that Pārśva quell the sins 'of the poet Māṇatuṅga' (kaïṇo ya māṇatuṅgassa). The same Mānatuṅga is by tradition credited with the Bhaktāmara Stotra. The hymn is composed in Prakrit (Jaina Māhārāṣṭrī / Ardhamāgadhī colouring) in āryā-type gāthās. Its alternate names — Bhayahara Stotra ('fear-remover') and Namiūṇa Stotra (from its opening word namiūṇa, 'having bowed') — both derive from the text. Note: the attribution rests on the in-text colophon and tradition rather than on an external dated record; see notes.
structureArc: v.1 is the invocation: bowing to the feet of Pārśva — radiant with the crest-jewel rays of homaging gods — the poet announces the 'saṁstava' that destroys great fear. The body of the hymn is a litany of seven great dangers, each developed in a verse-pair on the same template (the calamity is painted, then the devotee who remembers Pārśva is delivered): disease/leprosy (vv.2–3, with the simile of fire-scorched mountains regaining greenery), shipwreck at sea (vv.4–5), forest-fire (vv.6–7), the venomous serpent (vv.8–9, where Pārśva's name-syllables make deadly venom seem a mere insect), bandits in the wilderness (vv.10–11), the rutting elephant — extending to the lion (vv.12–15), and battle (vv.16–17). v.18 gathers the whole list — disease, water, fire, serpent, thief, foe, lion, elephant, war — and declares that merely reciting Pārśva's name stills them all. v.19 praises the stotra itself as a treasure of blessings; vv.20–21 widen its scope to fears of kings, demons, bad dreams, ill omens, planets, twilight, road and night, and seal the poem with the poet Māṇatuṅga's signature. v.22 recalls the Kamaṭha legend and hails the victorious Pārśva; v.23 points to the secret eighteen-syllable mantra embedded 'in the middle' of the hymn; v.24 salutes the Dharaṇendra-worshipped Pārśva who destroys all calamities; v.25 closes with the poet's personal devotion — that even Pārśva's name equals a mantra.
recurringImagery: The organizing motif is the catalogue of 'great fears' (mahābhaya) — close to the classical Jain set of seven/eight perils (disease, water, fire, serpent/poison, robbers, wild beasts such as lion and elephant, captivity/king, and battle). Every danger-verse follows one fixed pattern: first the peril is rendered in concrete, sensory terror (storm-lashed waves, wind-whipped flames, a hooded cobra, tusks 'white as the moon, like pestles'), then the turn — 'but those who remember / bow to / bear as a weapon Pārśva's feet or name' pass through untouched. Two refrains recur as the means of rescue: the 'feet' of Pārśva (calaṇa-juala, the pair of feet one takes refuge in) and his 'name' (nāma), whose very syllables act as a perfected mantra. A repeated nature-simile reinforces the theme of restoration after ruin — the burnt mountain or forest that grows green again (v.3) — figuring the devotee who regains beauty, wealth and safety. These images are explained here once; each verse simply invokes them.
keyThemes: the seven/eight great worldly dangers and deliverance from each
the power of remembering (smaraṇa) the Jina's feet and name
the Kamaṭha upasarga and Pārśva's unshaken meditation
protective recitation for safety and well-being

glossary.definition: a hymn of sustained praise; the poem calls itself a saṁstava that destroys great fear.
'fear-remover'; the epithet that gives the stotra its popular name.
'great fear / great peril' — the life-threatening dangers (disease, drowning, fire, snakebite, etc.) catalogued through the hymn.
remembrance / protective recollection; also the term for each of the Nine Smaraṇas, the fixed protective stotras of the Pakkhī.
the 23rd Tīrthaṅkara, to whom this hymn is addressed; identified by the serpent-hood and by Dharaṇendra's protection.
'the pair of feet' (Skt. caraṇa-yugala); the Jina's feet as the object of refuge, repeated across the danger-verses.
a venom-bearer, i.e. a serpent (Prakrit visahara); the danger of snakebite faced in vv.8–9 and listed in v.18.
an affliction or hostile ordeal directed at an ascetic — here Kamaṭha's attack on the meditating Pārśva (v.22).
calamity or disturbance; v.24 says all upadravas are destroyed by Pārśva's power.
the demon (asura) whose storm-and-flood assault Pārśva endured without breaking his meditation (v.22).
the serpent-lord (nāga yakṣa) who sheltered Pārśva with his hoods and venerates him (v.24).
a sacred efficacious formula; the hymn treats Pārśva's name-syllables as a mantra and conceals a special one within itself.
the 'eighteen-syllable' mantra said in v.23 to lie hidden in the middle of the stotra, meditated on by those who know it.
'victor'; a Tīrthaṅkara who has conquered the passions — here Pārśva-jina.
a Prakrit verse in the āryā family of metres; the unit in which all 25 verses are composed.

howToRead: A newcomer can read this as a reassuring catalogue of dangers and rescues: notice the repeating pattern 'here is a terror — but those who remember Pārśva are saved,' and let the imagery carry the devotional point. An advanced practitioner can attend to the embedded eighteen-syllable mantra signposted in v.23, the precise sequence of the great-fears, and the colophon's naming of Māṇatuṅga, recognising the hymn's dual life as both public stotra and esoteric mantra-vehicle.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: ritual placement of the Namiūṇa as the 5th of the Nine Smaraṇas within the Pakkhī, and the verbatim verse text

sources.title: Bhaktamara Stotra (Wikipedia) — on Ācārya Mānatuṅga
sources.consultedFor: traditional attribution of fear-removing stotras to Ācārya Mānatuṅga

sources.title: Parshvanatha (Wikipedia)
sources.consultedFor: Pārśva as 23rd Tīrthaṅkara, the Kamaṭha upasarga and Dharaṇendra protection legend

notes: The attribution to Mānatuṅgasūri rests on the hymn's own colophon (v.21, 'kaïṇo ya māṇatuṅgassa') and on long-standing tradition that links it to the Bhaktāmara author; some scholars distinguish more than one Mānatuṅga, so the exact identity and date are not externally fixed — treat the authorship as traditional rather than documentarily certain. The eighteen-syllable mantra (v.23) is, by design, not spelled out in the verse and is transmitted via guru-paramparā; this preface does not reconstruct it. Verse and word readings (including spellings such as 'visahara', 'calaṇa', and the doubled/half conjuncts) follow the source book verbatim and may differ from other recensions; the verse count here is 25 per the supplied text. Where a danger-verse's grammar is dense or the simile compressed, individual-verse commentaries should flag any residual uncertainty rather than over-specify.
